name: ginga

metadata.name: ginga
metadata.summary: A scientific image viewer and toolkit

documentation:
Ginga is a toolkit designed for building viewers for scientific image
data in Python, visualizing 2D pixel data in numpy arrays.
The Ginga toolkit centers around an image display class which supports
zooming and panning, color and intensity mapping, a choice of several
automatic cut levels algorithms and canvases for plotting scalable
geometric forms.  In addition to this widget, a general purpose
'reference' FITS viewer is provided, based on a plugin framework.

Ginga is distributed under an open-source BSD licence. Please see the
file LICENSE.txt in the top-level directory for details.

classes.name: ginga.events.UIEvent

classes.name: ginga.events.KeyEvent
documentation:
A key press or release event in a Ginga viewer.

Attributes
----------
key : str
    The key as it is known to Ginga

state: str
    'down' if a key press, 'up' if a key release

mode : str
    The mode name of the mode that was active when the event happened

modifiers : set of str
    A set of names of modifier keys that were pressed at the time

data_x : float
    X part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

data_y : float
    Y part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

viewer : subclass of `~ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase`
    The viewer in which the event happened

classes.name: ginga.events.PointEvent
documentation:
A mouse/pointer/cursor event in a Ginga viewer.

Attributes
----------
button : str
    The name of the button as set up in the configuration

state: str
    'down' if a press, 'move' if being dragged, 'up' if a release

mode : str
    The mode name of the mode that was active when the event happened

modifiers : set of str
    A set of names of modifier keys that were pressed at the time

data_x : float
    X part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

data_y : float
    Y part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

viewer : subclass of `~ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase`
    The viewer in which the event happened

classes.name: ginga.events.ScrollEvent
documentation:
A mouse or trackpad scroll event in a Ginga viewer.

Attributes
----------
button : str
    The name of the button as set up in the configuration

state: str
    Always 'scroll'

mode : str
    The mode name of the mode that was active when the event happened

modifiers : set of str
    A set of names of modifier keys that were pressed at the time

direction : float
    A direction in compass degrees of the scroll

amount : float
    The amount of the scroll

data_x : float
    X part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

data_y : float
    Y part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

viewer : subclass of `~ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase`
    The viewer in which the event happened

classes.name: ginga.events.PinchEvent
documentation:
A pinch event in a Ginga viewer.

Attributes
----------
button : str
    The name of the button as set up in the configuration

state: str
    'start' (gesture starting), 'move' (in action) or 'stop' (done)

mode : str
    The mode name of the mode that was active when the event happened

modifiers : set of str
    A set of names of modifier keys that were pressed at the time

rot_deg : float
    Amount of rotation in degrees

scale : float
    Scale of the pinch shrink or enlargement

data_x : float
    X part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

data_y : float
    Y part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

viewer : subclass of `~ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase`
    The viewer in which the event happened

classes.name: ginga.events.PanEvent
documentation:
A pinch event in a Ginga viewer.

Attributes
----------
button : str
    The name of the button as set up in the configuration

state: str
    'start' (gesture starting), 'move' (in action) or 'stop' (done)

mode : str
    The mode name of the mode that was active when the event happened

modifiers : set of str
    A set of names of modifier keys that were pressed at the time

delta_x : float
    Amount of scroll movement in the X direction

delta_y : float
    Amount of scroll movement in the Y direction

data_x : float
    X part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

data_y : float
    Y part of the data coordinates of the viewer under the cursor

viewer : subclass of `~ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase`
    The viewer in which the event happened

methods.name: ginga.GingaPlugin.BasePlugin.start
documentation:
This method is called to start the plugin.
It is called after build_gui().
def build_gui(self, container):
If a plugin defines this method, it will be called with a
container object in which to build its GUI. It should finish
by packing into this container.  This will be called every
time the plugin is activated.
pass

Called to set the order of a multi-channel image.
The order should be determined by the loader, but this will
make a best guess if passed `order` is `None`.

methods.name: ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage.get_header
documentation: kwargs is needed so subclasses can interoperate with optional keywords.

methods.name: ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage.cutout_data
documentation:
Cut out data area based on bounded coordinates.

Parameters
----------
x1, y1 : int
    Coordinates defining the minimum corner to be cut out

x2, y2 : int
    Coordinates *one greater* than the maximum corner

xstep, ystep : int
    Step values for skip intervals in the cutout region

z : int
    Value for a depth (slice) component for color images

astype :

Note that the coordinates for `x2`, `y2` are *outside* the
cutout region, similar to slicing parameters in Python.

methods.name: ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage.cutout_adjust
documentation:
Like `cutout_data`, but adjusts coordinates `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`
to be inside the data area if they are not already.  It tries to
preserve the width and height of the region, so e.g. (-2, -2, 5, 5)
could become (0, 0, 7, 7)

methods.name: ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage.cutout_cross
documentation:
Cut two data subarrays that have a center at (x, y) and with
radius (radius) from (image).  Returns the starting pixel (x0, y0)
of each cut and the respective arrays (xarr, yarr).

methods.name: ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage.get_shape_mask
documentation: Return full mask where True marks pixels within the given shape.

methods.name: ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage.get_shape_view
documentation:
Calculate a bounding box in the data enclosing `shape_obj` and
return a view that accesses it and a mask that is True only for
pixels enclosed in the region.

If `avoid_oob` is True (default) then the bounding box is clipped
to avoid coordinates outside of the actual data.

methods.name: ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage.cutout_shape
documentation:
Cut out and return a portion of the data corresponding to `shape_obj`.
A masked numpy array is returned, where the pixels not enclosed in
the shape are masked out.

methods.name: ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage.get_scaled_cutout2
documentation:
Extract a region of the image defined by points `p1` and `p2`
 and scale it by scale factors `scales`.

`method` describes the method of interpolation used, where the
default "basic" is nearest neighbor.

methods.name: ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage.get_pixels_on_line
documentation:
Uses Bresenham's line algorithm to enumerate the pixels along
a line.
(see http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bresenham%27s_line_algorithm)

If `getvalues`==False then it will return tuples of (x, y) coordinates
instead of pixel values.

methods.name: ginga.canvas.CanvasMixin.CanvasMixin.subcanvas_updated_cb
documentation:
This is a notification that a subcanvas (a canvas contained in
our canvas) has been modified.  We in turn signal that our canvas
has been modified.

A CanvasMixin is combined with the CompoundMixin to make a
tag-addressible canvas-like interface.  This mixin should precede the
CompoundMixin in the inheritance (and so, method resolution) order.

This module handles the mapping of user interface events (keyboard,
mouse, trackpad, gestures) to operations on a Ginga viewer.

classes.name: ginga.Bindings.BindingMapper

methods.name: ginga.Bindings.BindingMapper.map_button
documentation:
For remapping the buttons to different names. 'btncode' is a
fixed button code and 'alias' is a logical name.

methods.name: ginga.Bindings.BindingMapper.mode_key_down
documentation:
This method is called when a key is pressed and was not handled
by some other handler with precedence, such as a subcanvas.

methods.name: ginga.Bindings.BindingMapper.mode_key_up
documentation:
This method is called when a key is pressed in a mode and was
not handled by some other handler with precedence, such as a
subcanvas.

Map physical events to logical events.

The BindingMapper class maps physical events (key presses, button
clicks, mouse movement, etc) into logical events.  By registering for
logical events, plugins and other event handling code doesn't need to
care about the physical controls bindings.  The bindings can be changed
and everything continues to work.

methods.name: ginga.canvas.CanvasObject.CanvasObjectBase.within_radius
documentation:
Points `points` and point `pt` are in data coordinates.
Return True for points within the circle defined by
a center at point `pt` and within canvas_radius.
The distance between points is scaled by the canvas scale.

methods.name: ginga.canvas.CanvasObject.CanvasObjectBase.get_pt
documentation:
Takes an array of points `points` and a target point `pt`.
Returns the first index of the point that is within the
radius of the target point.  If none of the points are within
the radius, returns None.

methods.name: ginga.canvas.CanvasObject.CanvasObjectBase.within_line
documentation:
Points `points` and line endpoints `p_start`, `p_stop` are in
data coordinates.
Return True for points within the line defined by a line from
p_start to p_end and within `canvas_radius`.
The distance between points is scaled by the viewer's canvas scale.

methods.name: ginga.canvas.CanvasObject.CanvasObjectBase.get_center_pt
documentation: Return the geometric average of points as data_points.

methods.name: ginga.canvas.CanvasObject.CanvasObjectBase.get_move_scale_rotate_pts
documentation:
Returns 3 edit control points for editing this object: a move
point, a scale point and a rotate point.  These points are all in
data coordinates.

methods.name: ginga.canvas.CanvasObject.CanvasObjectBase.get_bbox
documentation:
Get bounding box of this object.

Returns
-------
(p1, p2, p3, p4): a 4-tuple of the points in data coordinates,
beginning with the lower-left and proceeding counter-clockwise.

This is the abstract base class for a CanvasObject.  A CanvasObject
is an item that can be placed on a Ginga canvas.

This class defines common methods used by all such objects.

classes.name: ginga.canvas.types.utils.ModeIndicator
documentation:
Shows a mode indicator.

NOTE: to get this to work properly, you need to add a callback to your
viewer's bindmapper like so:

    bm = viewer.get_bindmap()
    bm.add_callback('mode-set', lambda *args: viewer.redraw(whence=3))

classes.name: ginga.canvas.types.utils.FocusIndicator
documentation:
Shows an indicator that the canvas has the keyboard/mouse focus.
This is shown by a rectangle around the perimeter of the window.

NOTE: to get this to work properly, you need to add a callback to your
viewer like so:

    viewer.add_callback('focus', focus_obj.focus_cb)

classes.name: ginga.canvas.types.astro.AnnulusP

methods.name: ginga.canvas.types.astro.AnnulusP.sync_state
documentation: Called to synchronize state (e.g. when parameters have changed).

Special compound object to handle annulus shape that
consists of two objects with the same centroid.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> tag = canvas.add(Annulus(100, 200, 10, width=5, atype='circle'))
>>> obj = canvas.get_object_by_tag(tag)
>>> arr_masked = image.cutout_shape(obj)
```

classes.name: ginga.canvas.types.astro.Annulus2RP

methods.name: ginga.canvas.types.astro.Annulus2RP.sync_state
documentation: Called to synchronize state (e.g. when parameters have changed).

Special compound object to handle annulus shape that
consists of two objects, (one center point, plus two radii).

# Examples

```pycon
>>> tag = canvas.add(Annulus2R(100, 200, 10, 20, width=5, atype='box'))
>>> obj = canvas.get_object_by_tag(tag)
>>> arr_masked = image.cutout_shape(obj)
```

methods.name: ginga.canvas.types.plots.XYPlot.get_limits
documentation:
Get the limits of the data or the visible part of the plot.

If `lim_type` == 'data' returns the limits of all the data points.
Otherwise returns the limits of the visibly plotted area.  Limits
are returned in the form ((xmin, ymin), (xmax, ymax)), as an array.

methods.name: ginga.canvas.types.plots.XYPlot.draw
documentation:
Draw the plot.  Normally not called by the user, but by the viewer
as needed.

Plotable object that defines a single path representing an X/Y line plot.

Like a Path, but has some optimization to reduce the actual numbers of
points in the path, depending on the scale and pan of the viewer.

Plotable object that defines the plot background.

Can include a warning line and an alert line.  If the last Y value
plotted exceeds the warning line then the background changes color.
For example, you might be plotting detector values and want to set
a warning if a certain threshold is crossed and an alert if the
detector has saturated (alerts are higher than warnings).

classes.name: ginga.canvas.types.layer.CompoundObject
documentation:
Compound object on a Ginga canvas.
Parameters are the child objects making up the compound object.
Objects are drawn in the order listed. Example:

% code-block: Python
%
% CompoundObject(Point(x, y, radius, ...),
%                Circle(x, y, radius, ...))

This makes a point inside a circle.

classes.name: ginga.canvas.types.layer.Canvas
documentation: Class to handle canvas in Ginga.

classes.name: ginga.canvas.types.layer.DrawingCanvas
documentation: Drawing canvas.

classes.name: ginga.canvas.types.layer.ConstructedCanvas
documentation:
Constructed canvas from a list of specifications.

Parameters are specifications of child objects, where each specification
is a map specifying 'type' and (optionally) 'args' and 'kwargs'.
If present, 'args' is a sequence of arguments and 'kwargs' is a map
of keyword arguments to provide to the constructor of the child.

Example:

% code-block: Python
%
% ConstructedCanvas([dict(type='point', args=(x, y, radius),
%                         kwargs=dict(color='red')),
%                    dict(type='circle', args=(x, y, radius),
%                         kwargs=dict(color='yellow'))])

This makes a point inside a circle.

documentation:
Get lower-left and upper-right coordinates of the bounding box
of this compound object.

Returns
-------
x1, y1, x2, y2: a 4-tuple of the lower-left and upper-right coords

A CompoundMixin is a mixin class that makes an object that is an
aggregation of other objects.

It is used to make generic compound drawing types as well as (for example)
layers of canvases on top of an image.

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.set_window_size
documentation:
Report the size of the window to display the image.

**Callbacks**

Will call any callbacks registered for the `'configure'` event.
Callbacks should have a method signature of:

```
(viewer, width, height, ...)
```

:::{note}
This is called by the subclass with `width` and `height`
as soon as the actual dimensions of the allocated window are known.
:::

# Parameters

width

: The width of the window in pixels.

height

: The height of the window in pixels.

    Optimization flag that reduces the time to refresh the
    viewer by only recalculating what is necessary:

        0: New image, pan/scale has changed
        1: Cut levels or similar has changed
        2: Color mapping has changed
        2.3: ICC profile has changed
        2.5: Transforms have changed
        2.6: Rotation has changed
        3: Graphical overlays have changed

defined for the channel in preferences.
:::

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.zoom_fit
documentation:
Zoom to fit display window.
Pan the image and scale the view to fit the size of the set
limits (usually set to the image size).  Parameter `axis` can
be used to set which axes are allowed to be scaled; if set to
'lock' then all axes are scaled in such a way as to keep the
scale factor uniform between axes.  Also see {meth}`zoom_to`.

# Parameters

axis

: One of: 'x', 'y', 'xy', or 'lock' (default).

no_reset

: Do not reset `autozoom` setting.

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.panset_pct
documentation:
Similar to {meth}`set_pan`, except that pan positions
are determined by multiplying data dimensions with the given
scale factors, where 1 is 100%.

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.calc_pan_pct
documentation:
Calculate values for vertical/horizontal panning by percentages
from the current pan position.  Mostly used by scrollbar callbacks.

Parameters
----------
pad : int (optional, defaults to 0)
    a padding amount in pixels to add to the limits when calculating

min_pct : float (optional, range 0.0:1.0, defaults to 0.0)
max_pct : float (optional, range 0.0:1.0, defaults to 0.9)

Returns
-------
res : `~ginga.misc.Bunch.Bunch`
    calculation results, which include the following attributes:
    - rng_x : the range of X of the limits (including padding)
    - rng_y : the range of Y of the limits (including padding)
    - vis_x : the visually shown range of X in the viewer
    - vis_y : the visually shown range of Y in the viewer
    - thm_pct_x : the length of a X scrollbar arm as a ratio
    - thm_pct_y : the length of a Y scrollbar arm as a ratio
    - pan_pct_x : the pan position of X as a ratio
    - pan_pct_y : the pan position of Y as a ratio

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.pan_by_pct
documentation:
Pan by a percentage of the data space. This method is designed
to be called by scrollbar callbacks.

Parameters
----------
pct_x : float (range 0.0 : 1.0)
    Percentage in the X range to pan

pct_y : float (range 0.0 : 1.0)
    Percentage in the Y range to pan

pad : int (optional, defaults to 0)
    a padding amount in pixels to add to the limits when calculating

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.pan_omni
documentation:
Pan in a direction defined in degrees by an amount specified
as a percentage.

Parameters
----------
direction_deg : float (range 0.0 : 360.0)
    Direction in which to pan, where 0.0 is defined as

amount : float (range 0.0 : 1.0)
    Amount to distribute to X and Y according to direction

lock_x : bool (optional, defaults to False)
    If True, do not allow any panning in the X direction

lock_y : bool (optional, defaults to False)
    If True, do not allow any panning in the Y direction

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.pan_lr
documentation:
Pan left/right by an amount specified as a percentage of the
visible width.

Parameters
----------
pct_vw : float (range 0.0 : 1.0)
    Percent of visible width to pan

sign : int (1 or -1)
    -1 for left, 1 for right

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.pan_ud
documentation:
Pan up/down by an amount specified as a percentage of the
visible height.

Parameters
----------
pct_vh : float (range 0.0 : 1.0)
    Percent of visible height to pan

sign : int (1 or -1)
    -1 for up, 1 for down

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.position_at_canvas_xy
documentation:
Position a data point at a certain canvas position.
Calculates and sets the pan position necessary to position a
data point precisely at a point on the canvas.

# Parameters

data_pt

: data point to position, must include x and y (x, y).

canvas_pt

: canvas coordinate (cx, cy) where data point should end up.

no_reset

: See {meth}`set_pan`.

    This will be the one passed in, unless `output` is None

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase.get_rgb_image_as_buffer
documentation:
Get the current image shown in the viewer, with any overlaid

This can be overridden by subclasses.

Parameters
----------

    open python IO descriptor or None to have one created

format : str
    A string defining the format to save the image.  Typically
    at least 'jpeg' and 'png' are supported. (default: 'png')

quality: int
    The quality metric for saving lossy compressed formats.

Returns
-------
buffer : file IO-like object
    This will be the one passed in, unless `output` is None
    in which case a BytesIO obejct is returned

methods.name: ginga.ImageView.ImageViewBase._calc_font_size
documentation:
Heuristic to calculate an appropriate font size based on the
width of the viewer window.

Parameters
----------
win_wd : int
    The width of the viewer window.

Returns
-------
font_size : int
    Approximately appropriate font size in points

An abstract base class for displaying images represented by
Numpy data arrays.

This class attempts to do as much of the image handling as possible
using Numpy array manipulations (even color and intensity mapping)
so that only a minimal mapping to a pixel buffer is necessary in
concrete subclasses that connect to an actual rendering surface
(e.g., Qt, GTK, Tk, HTML5).

# Parameters

logger

: Logger for tracing and debugging. If not given, one will be created.

rgbmap

: RGB mapper object. If not given, one will be created.

settings

: Viewer preferences. If not given, one will be created.

methods.name: ginga.LayerImage.LayerImage.mono2color

Called in eventual response to a redraw request in the viewer.

methods.name: ginga.canvas.render.RendererBase.prepare_image
documentation:
Prepare an image to be displayed somewhere in the viewer.

Called at a point in the rendering process when it is known
that an image will need to be displayed.  In this renderer,
it is called via the "overlays" stage, and it creates an RGB
image in the cache which is then merged into the background.

Parameters
----------
cvs_img : instance of `~ginga.canvas.types.image.Image`
    The canvas object for the image to be displayed

cache : `~ginga.misc.Bunch.Bunch`
    A cache of items used by the renderer for a particular viewer

whence : `float` or `int`
    A number indicating at what level things need to be redrawn.

methods.name: ginga.opengl.Camera.Camera.orbit
documentation:
Causes the camera to "orbit" around the target point.
This is also called "tumbling" in some software packages.

methods.name: ginga.opengl.Camera.Camera.pan_delta
documentation:
This causes the scene to appear to translate right and up
(i.e., what really happens is the camera is translated left and down).
This is also called "panning" in some software packages.
Passing in negative delta values causes the opposite motion.

methods.name: ginga.opengl.Camera.Camera.track
documentation:
This causes the camera to translate forward into the scene.
This is also called "dollying" or "tracking" in some software packages.
Passing in a negative delta causes the opposite motion.

If push_target'' is True, the point of interest translates forward
(or backward) *with* the camera, i.e. it's "pushed" along with the
camera; otherwise it remains stationary.

if `adj_fov` is True then the camera's FOV is adjusted to keep the
target at the same size as before (so called "dolly zoom" or
"trombone effect").

modules.name: ginga.examples.matplotlib.example4_mpl
documentation:
$ ./example4_mpl.py [fits file]

A Ginga object rendering to a generic matplotlib Figure.  In short,
this allows you to have all the interactive UI goodness of a Ginga widget
window in a matplotlib figure.  You can interactively flip, rotate, pan, zoom,
set cut levels and color map warp a FITS image.  Furthermore, you can plot
using matplotlib plotting on top of the image and the plots will follow all
the transformations.

See the Ginga quick reference
(http://ginga.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickref.html)
for a list of the interactive features in the standard ginga widget.

example4 produces a simple matplotlib fits view with a couple of overplots.
This shows how you can use the functionality with straight python/matplotlib
sessions.  Run this by supplying a single FITS file on the command line.
! /usr/bin/env python
example4_mpl.py -- Load a fits file into a Ginga widget with a
matplotlib backend.
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.
Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.

modules.name: ginga.examples.matplotlib.example3_mpl
documentation:
$ ./example3_mpl.py [fits file]

example3 displays a native ginga widget beside a matplotlib figure as two
panes.  A fits file can be dropped into the left pane and manipulated using
the standard Ginga interactive controls
see (http://ginga.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickref.html).
Drop down boxes allow the color map to be changed.

The right pane has two buttons under it: pressing each button sets up a
different kind of plot in the mpl pane based on the current state of the
ginga pane.

You need Qt5/Qt6 with pyqt bindings (or pyside) installed to run this
example.
! /usr/bin/env python
example3_mpl.py -- Copy attributes from a Ginga Qt widget into a Matplotlib
figure.
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.

documentation:
$ ./example5_mpl.py [fits file]

This example program shows to capture button and keypress events
for your own use.  After loading a FITS file use the following keys:

Press 'x' to turn on capture of events and bypass most normal keystroke
processing. Press it again to resume normal processing.  An on-screen
message will tell you which mode you are in.

While in 'capture mode' you can draw points with the right mouse button.
Press 'c' to clear the canvas of drawn points.

modules.name: ginga.examples.gw.plot_time_series
documentation:
Plot time series data in an interactive plot viewer.

Usage
=====
$ python3 plot_time_series.py --loglevel=20 --stderr

Plots add new data every second, and update on screen every 5 sec.
24 hours of data is kept.  Each plot starts out in "autoaxis X PAN"
and "autoaxis Y VIS".

Things you can do in a plot:

1) Scroll the mouse wheel to zoom the X axis

2) Hold CTRL and scroll the mouse wheel to zoom the Y axis.

3) Press 'y' to autozoom the Y axis to the full range of the Y data.
   (The message "Autoaxis Y ON" should appear briefly).
   Press 'y' again to toggle off this behavior.

4) Press 'v' to autozoom the Y axis to the range of the *visible* data shown
   (The message "Autoaxis Y VIS" should appear briefly).
   Press 'v' again to toggle off this behavior.

5) Press 'x' to autozoom the X axis to the full range of the X data
   (The message "Autoaxis X ON" should appear briefly).
   Press 'x' again to toggle off this behavior.

6) Press 'p' to autopan the X axis to always show the latest data on
   the right (the message "Autoaxis X PAN" should appear briefly).
   Press 'p' again to toggle off this behavior.
.
! /usr/bin/env python3

classes.name: ginga.examples.gw.plot_time_series.FakeData
documentation: Generate fake time-series data.

modules.name: ginga.examples.gw.example1_video
documentation:
This example shows how you can set up a recurring refresh rate in Ginga.
It reads a video file and displays frames in a Ginga viewer.

Caveats:

1. There is no sound.  This is due to the lack of a decent python module
   that can read video files and provide \_both\_ audio and video streams.
2. Currently, it expects an OpenCV readable file as a command line parameter.
   Only formats supported by OpenCV can be used (typically JPEG encoded).

Requirements:

To run this example you will need the OpenCV bindings for Python installed.
This module lets us access the video stream of a video file frame-by-frame.

Usage:

```
$ example1_video.py [log options] --optimize <video file>
```

Workings:

Two threads are created: a GUI handling thread and a worker thread to
read frames from the file.  This allows the viewer to remain fairly
responsive to user actions.
! /usr/bin/env python
video_play.py -- video playback example with Ginga
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.
Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.

documentation:
Skeleton example of a Ginga global plugin called 'MyGlobalPlugin'

To enable it, copy it to your $HOME/.ginga/plugins folder (create it first
if it does not already exist), then run ginga with the command:

    $ ginga --modules=MyLocalPlugin

From the "Operations" menu you should be able to select
Custom->MyGlobalPlugin [G];
it should become active in the right panel.

classes.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyGlobalPlugin.MyGlobalPlugin

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyGlobalPlugin.MyGlobalPlugin.__init__
documentation:
This method is called when the plugin is loaded for the  first
time.  `fv` is a reference to the Ginga (reference viewer) shell.

You need to call the superclass initializer and then do any local
initialization.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyGlobalPlugin.MyGlobalPlugin.build_gui
documentation:
This method is called when the plugin is invoked.  It builds the
GUI used by the plugin into the widget layout passed as
`container`.
This method could be called several times if the plugin is opened
and closed.  The method may be omitted if there is no GUI for the
plugin.

This specific example uses the GUI widget set agnostic wrappers
to build the GUI, but you can also just as easily use explicit
toolkit calls here if you only want to support one widget set.

documentation: Callback from the reference viewer shell when a channel is added.
CALLBACKS

documentation: Callback from the reference viewer shell when a channel is deleted.

documentation:
Callback from the reference viewer shell when the focus changes
between channels.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyGlobalPlugin.MyGlobalPlugin.redo
documentation:
Called from the reference viewer shell when a new image has
been added to a channel.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyGlobalPlugin.MyGlobalPlugin.start
documentation:
This method is called just after `build_gui()` when the plugin
is invoked.  This method could be called more than once if the
plugin is opened and closed.  This method may be omitted
in many cases.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyGlobalPlugin.MyGlobalPlugin.stop
documentation:
This method is called when the plugin is stopped.
It should perform any special clean up necessary to terminate
the operation.  This method could be called more than once if
the plugin is opened and closed, and may be omitted if there is no
special cleanup required when stopping.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyGlobalPlugin.MyGlobalPlugin.__str__
documentation:
This method should be provided and should return the lower case
name of the plugin.

documentation:
Skeleton example of a Ginga local plugin called 'MyLocalPlugin'

To enable it, copy it to your $HOME/.ginga/plugins folder (create it first
if it does not already exist), then run ginga with the command:

    $ ginga --plugins=MyLocalPlugin

From the "Operations" menu you should be able to select
Custom->MyLocalPlugin; it should become active under the "Dialogs" tab.

classes.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin.__init__
documentation:
This method is called when the plugin is loaded for the  first
time.  `fv` is a reference to the Ginga (reference viewer) shell
and `fitsimage` is a reference to the specific CanvasView
object associated with the channel on which the plugin is being
invoked.
You need to call the superclass initializer and then do any local
initialization.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin.build_gui
documentation:
This method is called when the plugin is invoked.  It builds the
GUI used by the plugin into the widget layout passed as
`container`.
This method may be called many times as the plugin is opened and
closed for modal operations.  The method may be omitted if there
is no GUI for the plugin.

This specific example uses the GUI widget set agnostic wrappers
to build the GUI, but you can also just as easily use explicit
toolkit calls here if you only want to support one widget set.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin.close
documentation:
Example close method.  You can use this method and attach it as a
callback to a button that you place in your GUI to close the plugin
as a convenience to the user.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin.start
documentation:
This method is called just after `build_gui()` when the plugin
is invoked.  This method may be called many times as the plugin is
opened and closed for modal operations.  This method may be omitted
in many cases.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin.pause
documentation:
This method is called when the plugin loses focus.
It should take any actions necessary to stop handling user
interaction events that were initiated in `start()` or
`resume()`.
This method may be called many times as the plugin is focused
or defocused.  It may be omitted if there is no user event handling
to disable.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin.resume
documentation:
This method is called when the plugin gets focus.
It should take any actions necessary to start handling user
interaction events for the operations that it does.
This method may be called many times as the plugin is focused or
defocused.  The method may be omitted if there is no user event
handling to enable.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin.stop
documentation:
This method is called when the plugin is stopped.
It should perform any special clean up necessary to terminate
the operation.  The GUI will be destroyed by the plugin manager
so there is no need for the stop method to do that.
This method may be called many  times as the plugin is opened and
closed for modal operations, and may be omitted if there is no
special cleanup required when stopping.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin.redo
documentation:
This method is called when the plugin is active and a new
image is loaded into the associated channel.  It can optionally
redo the current operation on the new image.  This method may be
called many times as new images are loaded while the plugin is
active.  This method may be omitted.

methods.name: ginga.examples.reference-viewer.MyLocalPlugin.MyLocalPlugin.__str__
documentation:
This method should be provided and should return the lower case
name of the plugin.
import any other modules you want here--it's a python world!

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.AutoCutsBase.get_crop
documentation:
Get a cropped region of data from an image.

Parameters
----------
image : subclass of `~ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage`
    Image object from which the cut levels should be calculated

crop_radius : int (optional, default `None`)
    The radius of a crop region to extract from the image.
    If `None`, then the radius will default to the `crop_radius`
    attribute.

Returns
-------
result : ndarray
    The cropped data

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.AutoCutsBase.get_crop_data
documentation:
Get a cropped region of data from a ndarray.

Parameters
----------
data : ndarray
    Image data from which the data should be cropped

crop_radius : int (optional, default `None`)
    The radius of a crop region to extract from the image data.
    If `None`, then the radius will default to the `crop_radius`
    attribute.

Returns
-------
result : ndarray
    The cropped data

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.AutoCutsBase.get_full
documentation:
Get the full (or cropped) array of data from an image.

Parameters
----------
image : subclass of `~ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage`
    Image object from which the cut levels should be calculated

px_limit : int (optional, default `None`)
    The limit for extracting the full image data.  If the number
    of pixels in the data is larger than this value, a crop from
    the data will be used instead.

Returns
-------
result : ndarray
    The (possibly cropped) data

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.AutoCutsBase.get_sample
documentation:
Return a sample from the full data array of the passed image.

Parameters
----------
image : subclass of `~ginga.BaseImage.BaseImage`
    Image object from which the cut levels should be calculated

num_points : int (optional, default `None`)
    Specifies the number of points to sample.  If `None`, the number
    pixels will be calculated to a "reasonable representative sample".

Returns
-------
result : ndarray
    The sampled data

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.AutoCutsBase.get_sample_data
documentation:
Return a sample from a data array.

Parameters
----------
data : ndarray
    Image data from which the data should be sampled

num_points : int (optional, default `None`)
    Specifies the number of points to sample.  If `None`, the number
    pixels will be calculated to a "reasonable representative sample".

Returns
-------

    The sampled data

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.AutoCutsBase.cut_levels
documentation:
Apply the cut levels to data.

Parameters
----------
data_np : ndarray
    A numpy array of data to which the cut levels should be applied

loval : float
    The low cut level

hival : float
    The high cut level

vmin : float (optional, default 0.0)
    The floor of the range which is the output of the cut levels

vmax : float (optional, default 255.0)
    The ceiling of the range which is the output of the cut levels

Returns
-------
result : ndarray
    The result of applying the cut levels to the input array
Base class for auto cuts algorithms.

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.Histogram.calc_histogram
documentation: Internal function used by this class.

Calculate the cut levels based on a histogram analysis of the data.

# Parameters

logger

: Logger for tracing and debugging.

sample

: Specifies how to access the image for calculation:
  \- crop: crop an area from the middle of the image
  \- grid: sample data in a grid pattern across the image
  \- full: use the full image data

full_px_limit

: Specifies the limit for using the full data if sample == 'full'.
  If the number of pixels in the image is larger than this, then
  the image will fall back to using a crop.

num_points

: Specifies the number of points in the grid if sample == 'grid',
  or the diameter of the crop, if sample == 'crop' (or 'full' and
  number of pixels exceeds `full_px_limit`).  If None, the number
  pixels will be calculated to a "reasonable representative sample".

pct

: Specifies the percentage of the histogram bins to retain

numbins

: Specifies the number of bins used in calculating the histogram

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.StdDev.calc_stddev
documentation: Internal function used by this class.

Calculate the cut levels based on a standard deviation analysis of
the data.

The calculation is:

: loval = hensa_lo * sdev(sample_data) + mean(sample_data)
  hival = hensa_hi * sdev(sample_data) + mean(sample_data)

# Parameters

logger

: Logger for tracing and debugging.

sample

: Specifies how to access the image for calculation:
  \- crop: crop an area from the middle of the image
  \- grid: sample data in a grid pattern across the image
  \- full: use the full image data

full_px_limit

: Specifies the limit for using the full data if sample == 'full'.
  If the number of pixels in the image is larger than this, then
  the image will fall back to using a crop.

num_points

: Specifies the number of points in the grid if sample == 'grid',
  or the diameter of the crop, if sample == 'crop' (or 'full' and
  number of pixels exceeds `full_px_limit`).  If None, the number
  pixels will be calculated to a "reasonable representative sample".

hensa_lo

: Specifies the low cut multiplication factor to apply to the
  standard deviation before adding the median (usually \< 0)

hensa_hi

: Specifies the low cut multiplication factor to apply to the
  standard deviation before adding the median (usually > 0)

documentation: See subclass documentation.

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.MedianFilter.calc_cut_levels_data
documentation: See subclass documentation.

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.MedianFilter.calc_medianfilter
documentation: Internal function used by this class.

Calculate the cut levels based on a median filtering analysis of
the data.

The calculation is:

: out = median_filter(sample_data, size=length)
  loval, hival = min(out), max(out)

# Parameters

logger

: Logger for tracing and debugging.

num_points

: Specifies the number of points to sample making up the grid.
  If None, the number of points will be calculated to a
  "reasonable representative sample".

length

: Specifies the size of the median filter to apply to the data

methods.name: ginga.AutoCuts.ZScale.calc_zscale
documentation: Internal function used by this class.

Calculate the cut levels based on a median filtering analysis of
the data.

Based on STScI's numdisplay implementation of IRAF's ZScale.

The calculation is:

: local, hival = zscale(sample_data, contrast)

# Parameters

logger

: Logger for tracing and debugging.

contrast

: Specifies the contrast parameter to use in the zscale calculation

num_points

: Specifies the number of points to sample making up the grid.
  If None, the number of points will be calculated to a
  "reasonable representative sample".

documentation:

methods.name: ginga.util.io.io_base.BaseIOHandler.get_matching_indexes
documentation:
Parameters
----------

    A string in the form of a pair of brackets enclosing some

Returns
-------

modules.name: ginga.util.io.io_fits
documentation:
There are two possible choices for a python FITS file reading package
compatible with Ginga: astropy and fitsio.

To force the use of one programmatically, do:

    from ginga.util.io import io_fits
    io_fits.use('package')

(replace 'package' with one of {'astropy', 'fitsio'}) before you load
any images.  Otherwise Ginga will try to pick one for you.

Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.

functions.name: ginga.util.contour.get_contours
documentation:
Get sets of contour points for numpy array `data`.
`levels` is a sequence of values around which to calculate the
contours.  Returns a list of numpy arrays of points--each array
makes a polygon if plotted as such.

functions.name: ginga.util.contour.calc_contours
documentation:
Get sets of contour points for numpy array `data`.
`num_contours` specifies the number (int) of contours to make.
Returns a list of numpy arrays of points--each array makes a polygon
if plotted as such.

functions.name: ginga.util.contour.create_contours_obj
documentation:
Create and return a compound object for ginga `canvas`, consisting
of a number of contour polygons.  `contour_groups` is a list of
numpy arrays of points representing polygons, such as returned by
calc_contours() or get_contours().
`colors` (if provided) is a list of colors for each polygon.
Any other keyword parameters are passed on to the Polygon class.

methods.name: ginga.util.vip.ViewerImageProxy.get_shape_view
documentation:
Calculate a bounding box in the data enclosing `shape_obj` and
return a view that accesses it and a mask that is True only for
pixels enclosed in the region.

If `avoid_oob` is True (default) then the bounding box is clipped
to avoid coordinates outside of the actual data.

methods.name: ginga.util.vip.ViewerImageProxy.get_pixels_on_line
documentation:
Uses Bresenham's line algorithm to enumerate the pixels along
a line.
(see http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bresenham%27s_line_algorithm)

If `getvalues`==False then it will return tuples of (x, y) coordinates
instead of pixel values.

This class can be used in lieu of a `~ginga.BaseImage`-subclassed
object (such as `~ginga.AstroImage` to handle cases where either an
image has been loaded into a viewer in the conventional way, OR multiple
images have been plotted onto the viewer canvas.  It has a subset of the
API for an image wrapper object, and can be substituted for that in cases
that only use the subset.

Every `ImageView`-based viewer has one already constructed inside it.

Example. Previously, one might do:

   image = viewer.get_image()
   data, x1, y1, x2, y2 = image.cutout_radius(data_x, data_y, radius)

Alternative, using the already built vip:

    image = viewer.get_vip()
    data, x1, y1, x2, y2 = image.cutout_radius(data_x, data_y, radius)

Here are the methods that are supported:
* cutout_data
* cutout_adjust
* cutout_radius
* cutout_cross
* get_pixels_on_line
* get_pixels_on_curve
* get_shape_view
* cutout_shape
* get_size
* get_depth
* get_center
* get_minmax
* get_data_xy
* info_xy
* pixtoradec
* has_valid_wcs
* _slice

Supported properties:
* shape
* width
* height
* depth
* ndim

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.scale.Scale
documentation:
The Scale stage will scale a 2D image.

If 'Long Side' (`longside`) is set to some pixel size, then the image
is scaled so that the long side will be that many pixels; the other
side will be scaled to keep the aspect ratio.

If 'Long Side' is not set, or 'Scale' (`scale`) has been set to some
float value, then the image is scaled according to that scale.

'Interpolation' (`interp`) informs what kind of interpolation will be
done to the image during the scaling.

functions.name: ginga.util.mosaic.get_warp_indexes
documentation:
Get numpy index arrays to warp an image into another projection.

For every pixel coordinate for numpy array shape `shape_in`,
convert to wcs coordinates according to `wcs_in` and then back
to pixel coordinates according to `wcs_out`.

# Parameters

shape_in

: The shape of the array to be projected

wcs_in

: Ginga WCS wrapper that is associated with the input array

wcs_out

: Ginga WCS wrapper that is associated with the output

# Returns

Returns a 3-tuple (old_pts, coords, new_pts), where all three
arrays are Nx2

functions.name: ginga.util.mosaic.warp_image
documentation:
Warp image in 2D numpy array `data` to a new array.

Warps `data_in` using `wcs_in` and projecting into `wcs_out`.
The resulting array may have empty pixels which are initially filled
with `fill` and then computed from the median value of surrounding
pixels at a radius of `pixel_radius`.

# Parameters

data_in

: The array to be warped (projected)

wcs_in

: Ginga WCS wrapper that is associated with the input array

wcs_out

: Ginga WCS wrapper that is associated with the output

fill

: The value to initially fill the new output image array

pixel_radius

: The pixel radius to use for collecting values to fill empty pixels

# Returns

Returns a 5-tuple (data_out, old_pts, coords, new_pts, out_pts).
`data_out` is the warped image, with an alpha mask layer attached.
`out_pts` is a Nx2 array describing the relocated pixels in data_out.

See `get_warp_indexes` for discussion of the `old_pts`, `coords`
and `new_pts` return values.

methods.name: ginga.util.mosaic.ImageMosaicer.mosaic
documentation:
Create a mosaic of `images`.

Returns a `~ginga.AstroImage.AstroImage`

Class for creating mosaics in a `~ginga.AstroImage.AstroImage`.

Individual tiles are transformed and inserted into the right place
in the image ndarray.  The array can be automatically enlarged as
necessary to accomodate the new tiles.

Typical usage:

```pycon
>>> mosaicer = ImageMosaicer(logger)
>>> mosaicer.mosaic(images)
```

where `images` is a list of `~ginga.AstroImage.AstroImage` that
should be plotted in `viewer`.

methods.name: ginga.util.mosaic.CanvasMosaicer.mosaic
documentation:
Plot a mosaic of `images` in `viewer` on `canvas`.
If `canvas` is `None` the viewer's default canvas is used.

Class for creating collages on a Ginga canvas.

A collage is sort of like a mosaic, except that instead of creating a
large image array, individual tiles are transformed and plotted on a
canvas.

Typical usage:

```pycon
>>> collager = CanvasMosaicer(logger)
>>> collager.mosaic(viewer, images)
```

where `images` is a list of `~ginga.AstroImage.AstroImage` that
should be plotted in `viewer`.

modules.name: ginga.util.stages.color
documentation:
These stages are all used in implementing the RGB mapping pipeline.
See ginga.RGBMap and ginga.util.stages.render to see how they are used.

Generally speaking, the pipeline looks like

    RGBInput | Distribute | ShiftMap | IntensityMap | ColorMap
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.
Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.

methods.name: ginga.util.stages.color.RGBMapStage.set_bpp
documentation:
Set the bit depth (per band) for the output.
A typical "32-bit RGBA" image would be 8; a 48-bit image would
be 16, etc.

A class that all stages participating in color mapping subclass.

Establishes the bit depth of the RGB mapping.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.rotate.Rotate
documentation:
Rotate a 2D image.

'Rotate' (`rot_deg`) gives the angle of rotation for the image.

'Clip' (`clip`) tells whether to clip the image at its existing size,
or change size as needed to contain the entire image.

'Add alpha' (`add_alpha`) will add an alpha channel to keep the
parts of the expanded rotated image invisible.

functions.name: ginga.util.loader.load_data
documentation:
Load data from a file.

This call is used to load a data item from a filespec (path or URL)

Parameters
----------
filespec : str
    The path of the file to load (can be a URL).

idx : int or string (optional)
    The index or name of the data unit in the file (e.g. an HDU name)

logger : python logger (optional)
    A logger to record progress opening the item

All other keyword parameters are passed to the opener chosen for
the file type.

Returns
-------
data_obj : a data object for a ginga viewer

functions.name: ginga.util.loader.add_opener
documentation:
Add an opener to the registry of file openers.

Parameters
----------
opener : subclass of `~ginga.util.io.io_base.BaseIOHandler`
    a class that implements an opener

mimetypes : list of str
    a sequence of the MIME types this opener can handle

priority : int
    a priority that ranks this opener compared to others registered
    for the same MIME types.  The lower the number (negative ok) the
    higher the priority.  Default: 0

note : str
    a short note that will be displayed to describe the opener
    in GUIs

modules.name: ginga.util.stages.render
documentation:
These pipeline stages are used in creating the StandardPipelineRenderer,
the default renderer for standard widget backends.

[createbg] => [overlays] => [iccprof] => [flipswap] => [rotate] => [output]
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.
Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.CreateBg

Create the background RGB image, sized to fit the area that needs to
be painted in the viewer, and big enough so there is room to rotate it.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.ICCProf
documentation:
Convert the given RGB data from the input ICC profile to the
output ICC profile registered for the viewer.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.FlipSwap
documentation: Flip or swap axes according the viewer settings.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.Rotate
documentation: Rotate the image according the viewer settings.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.Output
documentation:
Create the output image by trimming height and width and reordering
channels according to viewer settings.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.Overlays
documentation:
The Overlays stage constructs an embedded pipeline to process each
canvas into a RGBA image.  A pipeline is run for each image that
needs to be processed. The pipeline run depends on the type of image
on the canvas:

ginga.canvas.types.image.Image:

[scale] => [reorder] => [merge]

ginga.canvas.types.image.NormImage:

[scale] => [reorder] => [cuts] => [rgbmap] => [merge]

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.Scale
documentation:
Scale the image according to the viewers scale settings.  Only the
portions of the image that will be visible are copied and then scaled.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.Reorder
documentation:
Reorder RGB images for the pipeline.

Reorders multiband images for processing in the pipeline standard
order.  Additionally, if the image has an alpha channel, then strip
it off and save it until it is recombined later with the colorized
output--this saves us having to deal with an alpha band in the cuts
leveling and RGB mapping routines.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.Merge
documentation:
Merge the processed image into the result of our stage at the
appropriate offset and with the appropriate alpha blending.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.Cuts
documentation:
Process the cut levels (loval, hival) for the image.

This has the effect of mapping every input value between loval and
hival to the range specified by the output values (vmin, vmax).
Input values lower than loval are mapped to loval and values higher
than hival are mapped to hival.

classes.name: ginga.util.stages.render.RGBMap
documentation:
Color map an image using a RGBMap.

This color-maps an image according to the settings in the RGBMap
of the image (if it has one), or the viewer (if not).

classes.name: ginga.util.wcsmod.common.BaseWCS

methods.name: ginga.util.wcsmod.common.BaseWCS.load_header
documentation:
Initializes the internal package WCS from the keywords in
the WCS header.

Parameters
----------
header : dict-like
    A dictionary like object mapping WCS keywords to values.

fobj : object, optional
    A handle to the open object used to load the image.

This method is called to create and initialize a wrapped WCS
object from WCS keywords.  It typically will set self.header
as needed and from that initialize self.wcs

If the WCS creation fails, self.wcs should be set to None and
a WCSError raised.

The value of `fobj` depends on the method used to open the file,
but will usually be an open handle to the file (e.g. astropy.io.fits)

methods.name: ginga.util.wcsmod.common.BaseWCS.spectral_coord
documentation:
Map data (pixel) indexes into an element of the WCS coordinate.

Parameters
----------
idxs : tuple-like
    A sequence of indexes making up a data coordinate

coords : 'data' or None, optional, default to 'data'
    Expresses whether the data coordinate is indexed from zero

See `pixtoradec` for discussion of the `idxs` and `coords`
parameters.

Returns
-------
The spectral coordinate resolved from the wrapped WCS.

This is usually the WCS value in the third axis of the coordinate
system defined by the WCS for multidimensional (i.e. > 2D) data.

methods.name: ginga.util.wcsmod.common.BaseWCS.pixtoradec
documentation:
Map pixel indexes into a sky coordinate in the WCS system
defined by the header.

Parameters
----------
idxs : tuple-like
    A sequence of indexes making up a data coordinate

coords : 'data' or None, optional, default to 'data'
    Expresses whether the data coordinate is indexed from zero

This method returns part of the world coordinates for the data
(e.g. pixel) coordinate specified by `idxs`.

`idxs` is expressed as a sequence of floating point values,
usually representing (possibly fractional) pixel values.

The `coords` parameter should be consulted to determine whether
to increment the constituents of the data coordinate, depending
on the API for the wrapped WCS.  If coords == 'data', then the
indexes originate at 0, otherwise they can be assumed to originate
from 1.

Returns
-------
Returns a 2-tuple containing the WCS converted values in the
first two axes of the coordinate system defined by the WCS
(e.g. (ra_deg, dec_deg) as floats).

methods.name: ginga.util.wcsmod.common.BaseWCS.radectopix
documentation:
Map a sky coordinate in the WCS system defined by the header
into pixel indexes.

Parameters
----------
ra_deg : float
    First coordinate

dec_deg : float
    Second coordinate

coords : 'data' or None, optional, defaults to 'data'
    Expresses whether to return coordinates indexed from zero

naxispath : list-like or None, optional, defaults to None
    A sequence defining the pixel indexes > 2D, if any

This method returns the 2D data (e.g. pixel) coordinate for the
world coordinate defined by (ra_deg, dec_deg) + [0]*len(naxispath)

ra_deg and dec_deg are expressed in degrees (float) and prepended
to the naxispath, which is usually provided only for dimensions > 2D.

The `coords` parameter should be consulted to determine whether
to return the values of the data coordinates at origin 0 or 1.
If coords == 'data', then the values should be returned at 0
origin.

Returns
-------
Returns a 2-tuple containing the data (pixel) values in the
first two axes of the data coordinate system defined by the WCS
(e.g. (x, y) as floats).

methods.name: ginga.util.wcsmod.common.BaseWCS.pixtosystem
documentation:
Map pixel values into a sky coordinate in a named system.

Parameters
----------
idxs : tuple-like
    A sequence of indexes making up a data coordinate

system : str or None
    A string naming a coordinate system

coords : 'data' or None, optional, default to 'data'
    Expresses whether the data coordinate is indexed from zero

See `pixtoradec` for discussion of the `idxs` and `coords`
parameters.

`system` names a coordinate system in which the results are
expected.  For example, the wrapped WCS may express a coordinate
system in "fk5" and the `system` parameter is "galactic".

This call differs from pixtoradec() in that it may need to do
a secondary mapping from the image's default coordinate system
to the `system` one.

Returns
-------
Returns a 2-tuple containing the WCS converted values in the
first two axes of the coordinate system defined by `system`
(e.g. (ra_deg, dec_deg) as floats).

methods.name: ginga.util.wcsmod.common.BaseWCS.datapt_to_wcspt

# Returns

methods.name: ginga.util.wcsmod.common.BaseWCS.fix_bad_headers
documentation:
Fix up bad headers that cause problems for the wrapped WCS
module.

Subclass can override this method to fix up issues with the
header for problem FITS files.
Base class for WCS.

modules.name: ginga.util.wcsmod
documentation:
We are fortunate to have several possible choices for a python WCS package
compatible with Ginga: astlib, kapteyn, starlink and astropy.
kapteyn and astropy wrap Mark Calabretta's "WCSLIB", astLib wraps
Jessica Mink's "wcstools", and I'm not sure what starlink uses (their own?).

Note that astlib requires pyfits (or astropy) in order to create a WCS
object from a FITS header.

To force the use of one, do:

```python
from ginga.util import wcsmod
wcsmod.use('kapteyn')
```

before you load any images.  Otherwise Ginga will try to pick one for
you.

Note that you can register custom WCS types using:

```python
from ginga.util.wcsmod.common import register_wcs
register_wcs('mywcs', MyWCSClass, list_of_coord_types)
```

Look at the implemented WCS wrappers for details.
wcsmod -- module wrapper for WCS calculations.
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.
Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.

documentation:
Generate config file documentation for a given config name.

If found, it will be a Python code block of the contents.
If not found, it will have a generic message that the config
is not available.

# Parameters

config_name

: Config name that is attached to the configuration file.
  This is the same as input for `prefs.createCategory()`.
  For example, `'general'`, `'channel_Image'`, or
  `'plugin_Zoom'`.

cfgpath

: Where it is within package data.

kwargs

: Optional keywords for {func}`~astropy.utils.data.get_pkg_data_contents`.

# Returns

docstr

: Docstring to be inserted into documentation.

classes.name: ginga.util.toolbox.ModeIndicator
documentation:
This class adds a mode status indicator to a viewer's lower right-hand
corner.

Usage:
Instantiate this class with a Ginga ImageView{Toolkit} object as the
sole constructor argument.  Save a reference to the mode indicator
object somewhere so it doesn't get collected.

NOTE: Please don't use this class--it's likely to be deprecated!
Instead, use the show_mode_indicator() method provided by the
ImageView class.

xc, yc : int
    X and Y indices of the approximate center.

radius : float
    Half-width of the region to consider around the given center.

Returns
-------
x, y : float
    Centroid indices.

Raises
------
IQCalcError
    Missing dependency.

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.get_threshold
documentation:
Calculate threshold for {meth}`find_bright_peaks`.

# Parameters

data

: Data array.

sigma

: Sigma for the threshold.

# Returns

threshold

: Threshold based on good data, its median, and the given sigma.
FINDING BRIGHT PEAKS

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.find_bright_peaks
documentation:
Find bright peak candidates in in the given data.

# Parameters

data

: Input data to find peaks from.

threshold

: Detection threshold. Below this value, an object is not
  considered a candidate. If not given, a default is calculated
  using {meth}`get_threshold` with the given `sigma`.

sigma

: Sigma for the threshold.

radius

: Pixel radius for determining local maxima. If the
  desired objects are larger in size, specify a larger radius.

# Returns

peaks

: A list of candidate object coordinate tuples `(x, y)` in data.

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.cut_region
documentation:
Return a cut region.

Parameters
----------
x, y : int
    Indices of central pixel.

radius : int
    Half-width in both X and Y directions.

data : array-like
    Data array to cut from.

Returns
-------
x0, y0 : int
    Origin of the region.

arr : array-like
    Cut region (a view, not copy).

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.cut_cross
documentation:
Cut data vertically and horizontally at the given position
with the given radius.

Parameters
----------
x, y : int
    Indices where vertical and horizontal cuts meet.

radius : float
    Radius of both cuts.

data : array-like
    Data array to cut from.

Returns
-------
x0 : array-like
    Starting pixel of horizontal cut (in X).

y0 : array-like
    Starting pixel of vertical cut (in Y).

xarr : array-like
    Horizontal cut (in X).

yarr : array-like
    Vertical cut (in Y).

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.brightness
documentation:
Return the brightness value found in a region defined by input
location and radius. Region is cut using {meth}`cut_region`.

# Parameters

x, y

: Indices of central pixel.

radius

: Half-width in both X and Y directions.

medv

: Background to subtract off.

data

: Data array.

# Returns

res

: Brightness.

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.fwhm_data

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.ensquared_energy

Return a function of ensquared energy across pixel indices.

Encircled and ensquared energies (EE)

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.encircled_energy

Ideally, data is already a masked array and is assumed to be centered.
This is adapted from poppy package. See licenses/POPPY_LICENSE.md .

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.evaluate_peaks
documentation:
Evaluate photometry for given peaks in data array.

# Parameters

peaks

: List of `(x, y)` tuples containing indices of peaks.

data

: Data array that goes with the given peaks.

fwhm_radius, fwhm_method

: See {meth}`get_fwhm`.

ee_total_radius

: Radius, in pixels, where encircled and ensquared energy fractions
  are defined as 1.

cb_fn

: If applicable, provide a callback function that takes a
  `ginga.misc.Bunch.Bunch` containing the result for each peak.
  It should not return anything.

ev_intr

: For threading, if applicable.

:::{note}
unused parameter `bright_radius` was removed in
release 4.0
:::

# Returns

objlist

: A list of successful results for the given peaks.
  Each result contains the following keys:

  - `objx`, `objy`: Fitted centroid from {meth}`get_fwhm`.
  - `pos`: A measure of distance from the center of the image.
  - `oid_x`, `oid_y`: Center-of-mass centroid from {meth}`centroid`.
  - `fwhm_x`, `fwhm_y`: Fitted FWHM from {meth}`get_fwhm`.
  - `fwhm`: Overall measure of fwhm as a single value.
  - `fwhm_radius`: Input FWHM radius.
  - `brightness`: Average peak value based on {meth}`get_fwhm` fits.
  - `elipse`: A measure of ellipticity.
  - `x`, `y`: Input indices of the peak.
  - `skylevel`: Sky level estimated from median of data array and
    `skylevel_magnification` and `skylevel_offset` attributes.
  - `background`: Median of the input array.
  - `ensquared_energy_fn`: Function of ensquared energy for different pixel radii.
  - `encircled_energy_fn`: Function of encircled energy for different pixel radii.
EVALUATION ON A FIELD

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc._sortkey
documentation: For sorting of result in {meth}`objlist_select`.

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.objlist_select
documentation:
Filter output from {meth}`evaluate_peaks`.

# Parameters

objlist

: Output from {meth}`evaluate_peaks`.

width, height

: Dimension of data array from which `objlist` was derived.

minfwhm, maxfwhm

: Limits for desired FWHM, where `(minfwhm, maxfwhm)`.

minelipse

: Minimum value of desired ellipticity (not inclusive).

edgew

: Factor between 0 and 1 that determines if a location is too close to the edge or not.

# Returns

results

: Elements of `objlist` that contain desired FWHM, ellipticity,
  and not too close to the edge.

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.pick_field
documentation:
Pick the first good object within the given field.

# Parameters

data

: Data array of the field.

peak_radius, threshold

: See {meth}`find_bright_peaks`.

fwhm_radius, ee_total_radius

: See {meth}`evaluate_peaks`.

minfwhm, maxfwhm, minelipse, edgew

: See {meth}`objlist_select`.

:::{note}
unused parameter `bright_radius` was removed in
release 4.0
:::

# Returns

result

: This is a single element of `objlist` as described in
  {meth}`evaluate_peaks`.

# Raises

IQCalcError

: No object matches selection criteria.

methods.name: ginga.util.iqcalc.IQCalc.qualsize

x1, y1, x2, y2

radius, threshold

minfwhm, maxfwhm, minelipse, edgew

:::{note}
unused parameter `bright_radius` was removed in
release 4.0
:::

# Returns

: This is a single element of `objlist` as described in

Class to handle model fitting and FWHM calculations.

documentation: This is where things get interesting.  With this projection,
straight lines in data space become curves in display space.
This is done by interpolating new values between the input
values of the data.  Since ``transform`` must not return a
differently-sized array, any transform that requires
changing the length of the data array must happen within
``transform_path``.
The base Ginga transform.
Now, the transforms themselves.

script developed by matplotlib developers.

functions.name: ginga.gtk4w.GtkHelp.pixbuf_new_from_file_at_size
documentation: def pixbuf_new_from_xpm_data(xpm_data):
xpm_data = bytes('\n'.join(xpm_data))
return GdkPixbuf.Pixbuf.new_from_xpm_data(xpm_data)
def pixbuf_new_from_array(data, rgbtype, bpp):
# NOTE: there is a bug in gtk4 with pixbuf_new_from_array()
# See: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/24062779/how-to-correctly-covert-3d-array-into-continguous-rgb-bytes/24070152#24070152
#return GdkPixbuf.Pixbuf.new_from_array(data, rgbtype, bpp)
height, width, depth = data.shape
pixl = GdkPixbuf.PixbufLoader.new_with_type('pnm')
# P6 is the magic number of PNM format,
# and 255 is the max color allowed
pixl.write((b"P6 %d %d 255 " % (width, height)) + data.tobytes(order='C'))
pix = pixl.get_pixbuf()
pixl.close()
return pix
def pixbuf_new_from_data(rgb_buf, rgbtype, hasAlpha, bpp, dawd, daht, stride):
return GdkPixbuf.Pixbuf.new_from_data(rgb_buf, rgbtype, hasAlpha, bpp,
dawd, daht, stride, None, None)

documentation: class CheckMenuItem(WidgetMask, Gtk.CheckMenuItem):
def __init__(self, *args, **kwdargs):
WidgetMask.__init__(self)
Gtk.CheckMenuItem.__init__(self, *args, **kwdargs)
def set_active(self, newval):
oldval = self.get_active()
if oldval != newval:
self.change()
super(CheckMenuItem, self).set_active(newval)

methods.name: ginga.aggw.CanvasRenderAgg.RenderContext.draw_line
documentation: NOTE: recent versions of aggdraw are not rendering Bezier curves
correctly--we comment out these temporarily so that we fall back to
a version that generates our own Beziers
def draw_bezier_curve(self, cp, line=None):
pen = None if line is None else line.render.pen
brush = None if fill is None else fill.render.brush
path = agg.Path()
path.moveto(cp[0][0], cp[0][1])
self.ctx.canvas.path(path, pen, brush)
def draw_ellipse_bezier(self, cp, line=None, fill=None):
# draw 4 bezier curves to make the ellipse because there seems
# to be a bug in aggdraw ellipse drawing function
pen = None if line is None else line.render.pen
brush = None if fill is None else fill.render.brush
path = agg.Path()
path.moveto(cp[0][0], cp[0][1])
self.ctx.canvas.path(path, pen, brush)

modules.name: ginga.ColorDist
documentation:
These algorithms are modeled after the ones described for ds9 here:

    http://ds9.si.edu/doc/ref/how.html
ColorDist.py -- Color Distribution algorithms
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.
Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.

methods.name: ginga.ColorDist.ColorDistBase.calc_hash
documentation: Create the hash table that implements the distribution function.

methods.name: ginga.ColorDist.ColorDistBase.get_dist_pct
documentation:
Calculate a domain value based on a percentage into the range.

Given a value between 0 and 1, calculate the value in the domain
that corresponds to this percentage of the distribution range.
This function is primarily used to build color bars for display.

Parameters
----------
pct : float
    A floating point value between 0 and 1

Returns
-------
val : float
    A value in the domain of the color distribution function

classes.name: ginga.ColorDist.LinearDist
documentation:
y = x
    where x in (0..1)

classes.name: ginga.ColorDist.LogDist
documentation:
y = log(a*x + 1) / log(a)
    where x in (0..1)

classes.name: ginga.ColorDist.PowerDist
documentation:
y = ((a ** x) - 1) / a
    where x in (0..1)

classes.name: ginga.ColorDist.SqrtDist
documentation:
y = sqrt(x)
    where x in (0..1)

classes.name: ginga.ColorDist.SquaredDist
documentation:
y = x ** 2
    where x in (0..1)

classes.name: ginga.ColorDist.AsinhDist
documentation:
y = asinh(nonlinearity * x) / factor
    where x in (0..1)

classes.name: ginga.ColorDist.SinhDist
documentation:
y = sinh(factor * x) / nonlinearity
    where x in (0..1)

classes.name: ginga.ColorDist.HistogramEqualizationDist

methods.name: ginga.ColorDist.HistogramEqualizationDist.hash_array
documentation: TODO: this method has a lot more overhead compared to the other
scaling methods because the hash array must be computed each time
the data is delivered to hash_array()--in the other scaling
methods it is precomputed in calc_hash().  Investigate whether
there is a way to make this more efficient.

The histogram equalization distribution function distributes colors
based on the frequency of each data value.

classes.name: ginga.ColorDist.CurveDist
documentation:
y = x
    where x in (0..1)

Undoes color map rotation and inversion, also resets contrast

methods.name: ginga.RGBMap.RGBMapper.reset_cmap

Similar to restore_cmap, but also restores the default color

methods.name: ginga.RGBMap.RGBMapper.get_rgb

methods.name: ginga.RGBMap.RGBMapper.get_rgbval

classes.name: ginga.RGBMap.NonColorMapper
documentation:
A kind of color mapper for data that is already in RGB form.

This mapper allows changing of color distribution and contrast
adjustment, but does no coloring.

classes.name: ginga.RGBMap.PassThruRGBMapper
documentation:
A kind of color mapper for data that is already in RGB form.

This mapper bypasses color distribution, contrast adjustment and
coloring.  It is thus the most efficient one to use for maximum
speed rendering of "finished" RGB data.

modules.name: ginga.trcalc
documentation: trcalc.py -- transformation calculations for image data
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.
Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.rotate_pt
documentation:
Rotate an array of points (x_arr, y_arr) by theta_deg offsetted
from a center point by (xoff, yoff).

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.rotate_clip
documentation:
Rotate numpy array `data_np` by `theta_deg` around rotation center
(rotctr_x, rotctr_y).  If the rotation center is omitted it defaults
to the center of the array.

No adjustment is done to the data array beforehand, so the result will
be clipped according to the size of the array (the output array will be
the same size as the input array).

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.get_scaled_cutout_wdhtdp_view
documentation:
Like get_scaled_cutout_wdht, but returns the view/slice to extract
from an image instead of the extraction itself.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.get_scaled_cutout_wdht
documentation:
Extract a region of the `data_np` defined by corners (x1, y1) and
(x2, y2) and resample it to fit dimensions (new_wd, new_ht).

`interpolation` describes the method of interpolation used, where the
default "basic" is nearest neighbor.  If `logger` is not `None` it will
be used for logging messages.  If `dtype` is defined then the output
array will be converted to that type; the default is the same as the
input type.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.get_scaled_cutout_basic_view
documentation:
Like get_scaled_cutout_basic, but returns the view/slice to extract
from an image, instead of the extraction itself

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.calc_image_merge_clip
documentation:
p1 (x1, y1, z1) and p2 (x2, y2, z2) define the extent of the (non-scaled)
data shown.  The image, defined by region q1, q2 is to be placed at dst
in the image (destination may be outside of the actual data array).

Refines the modified points (q1', q2') defining the clipped rectangle
needed to be cut from the source array and scaled.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.reorder_image
documentation:
Reorder src_arr, with order of color planes in src_order, as
dst_order.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.strip_z
documentation: Strips a Z component from `pts` if it is present.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.pad_z
documentation:
Adds a Z component from `pts` if it is missing.
The value defaults to `value` (0.0)

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.get_bounds
documentation:
Return the minimum point and maximum point bounding a
set of points.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.sort_xy
documentation: Sort a set of bounding box parameters.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.fill_array
documentation:
Fill array dstarr with a color value. order defines the color planes
in the array.  (r, g, b, a) are expected to be in the range 0..1 and
are scaled to the appropriate values.

dstarr can be a 2D or 3D array.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.make_filled_array
documentation:
Return a filled array with a color value. order defines the color
planes in the array.  (r, g, b, a) are expected to be in the range
0..1 and are scaled to the appropriate values.

shp can define a 2D or 3D array.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.remove_alpha
documentation:
Takes an array and removes an alpha layer from it if it has one.

Parameters
----------
arr : ndarray
    The input array

Returns
-------
new_arr, alpha_arr : both ndarray
    The input array with the alpha layer removed, and the alpha array
    None is returned for the alpha array if there was none.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.add_alpha
documentation:
Takes an array and adds an alpha layer to it if it doesn't already
exist.

Parameters
----------
arr : ndarray
    The input array

alpha : int, float, ndarray or None (optional)
    A single value or array of values for the alpha layer to be added

Returns
-------
new_arr : ndarray
    The input array with the alpha layer added.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.array_convert
documentation:
Convert an array from one datatype to another, preserving relative value.

Parameters
----------
arr_np : ndarray
    A numpy array of data

to_dtype : numpy dtype (e.g. np.dtype(np.uint16))
    the ndarray data type to convert to

Returns
-------
res_np : ndarray
   The converted array

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.cutout_data
documentation:
Cut out data area based on bounded coordinates.

Parameters
----------
x1, y1 : int
    Coordinates defining the minimum corner to be cut out

x2, y2 : int
    Coordinates *one greater* than the maximum corner

xstep, ystep : int
    Step values for skip intervals in the cutout region

z : int
    Value for a depth (slice) component for color images

astype :

Note that the coordinates for `x2`, `y2` are *outside* the
cutout region, similar to slicing parameters in Python.

functions.name: ginga.trcalc.cutout_adjust
documentation:
Like `cutout_data`, but adjusts coordinates `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`
to be inside the data area if they are not already.  It tries to
preserve the width and height of the region, so e.g. (-2, -2, 5, 5)
could become (0, 0, 7, 7)

modules.name: ginga.web.pgw.ipg
documentation:
This example illustrates using a Ginga widget in a web browser,  All the
rendering is done on the server side and the browser only acts as a display
front end.  Using this you could create an analysis type environment on a
server and view it via a browser.

See example usage with an ipython notebook at:

    https://gist.github.com/ejeschke/6067409

You will need a reasonably modern web browser with HTML5 canvas support.
Tested with Chromium 41.0.2272.76, Firefox 37.0.2, Safari 7.1.6
! /usr/bin/env python
ipg.py -- Module for simple FITS viewer in an HTML5 canvas web browser.
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.
Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.

modules.name: ginga.web.jupyterw.ImageViewJpw
documentation:
This example illustrates using a Ginga as the driver of a Jupyter web widget.

REQUIREMENTS:
  To use this code you will need the "ipywidgets" and "ipyevents" python
  modules installed.  These are easily installed via:

     $ pip install ipyevents
     $ jupyter nbextension enable --py --sys-prefix ipyevents

  or via conda:

     $ conda install -c conda-forge ipyevents

Basic usage in a Jupyter notebook:

import ipywidgets as widgets

# create a Jupyter image that will be our display surface
# format can be 'jpeg' or 'png'; specify width and height to set viewer size
jp_img = widgets.Image(format='jpeg', width=500, height=500)

# Boilerplate to create a Ginga viewer connected to this widget
# this could be simplified by creating a class that created viewers
# as a factory.
from ginga.misc.log import get_logger
logger = get_logger("v1", log_stderr=True, level=40)

from ginga.web.jupyterw.ImageViewJpw import EnhancedCanvasView
v1 = EnhancedCanvasView(logger=logger)
v1.set_widget(jp_img)

bd = v1.get_bindings()
bd.enable_all(True)

# You can now build a GUI with the image widget and other Jupyter
# widgets.  Here we just show the image widget.
v1.embed()
ImageViewJpw.py -- Module for a Ginga FITS viewer in a Jupyter web notebook.
This is open-source software licensed under a BSD license.
Please see the file LICENSE.txt for details.
